content:
Press enter or click to view image in full size

7 min read
Just now

--

Not tips. Not opinions. The actual before-and-after of five habits I rebuilt from scratch.

Switching to AI-assisted coding didn’t just make me faster. It quietly broke five habits I’d relied on for years — and forced me to build new ones to replace them.
This isn’t a listicle of vague productivity tips. Each section below is a real habit I used to have, why AI-assisted coding broke it, and the exact step-by-step process I now follow instead. If you’ve been coding with AI tools for a while and something feels slightly off in your workflow, one of these five is probably why.

1. I Stopped Reading Documentation Line by Line — And Started Reading It Backwards
What I used to do: Open the official docs for any new library, start at the introduction, read through examples in order, then start coding.
Why AI broke this: AI-generated code arrives already using specific methods and parameters. Reading documentation front-to-back after the fact felt redundant — I already had working code. But skipping documentation entirely meant I didn’t understand why the AI chose that particular approach, which came back to bite me during debugging.
What replaced it — step by step:
Step 1: Let the AI generate the code first
Step 2: Identify every method/function name that's
unfamiliar to you in the output
Step 3: Go to the official docs and search only for
those specific methods — not the whole page
Step 4: Read just the parameters and return values
for that one method
Step 5: Ask yourself: "would I have chosen this
approach over the alternatives listed nearby
in the docs?"This reverse approach takes a fraction of the time traditional documentation reading took, while still building real understanding of the code running in your project.

Press enter or click to view image in full size

2. I Stopped Writing Boilerplate — And Started Writing Specifications First
What I used to do: Start typing code directly. Boilerplate setup — Express server config, database connection, basic CRUD structure — got written by hand every single time, from memory, with small variations project to project.
Why AI broke this: AI generates boilerplate instantly and correctly almost every time. There’s genuinely no reason to type it manually anymore. But without a habit to replace it, I found myself accepting whatever boilerplate the AI produced without thinking about whether it actually fit my project’s specific needs.
What replaced it — step by step:
Step 1: Before generating any code, write a 3-5 line
specification of what the feature needs to do
Step 2: Include specific constraints:
"must use PostgreSQL, must include rate
limiting, must return paginated results"
Step 3: Generate the code against that specification,
not a vague one-line prompt
Step 4: Check the output against your original
specification line by line
Step 5: If something in the output wasn't in your
spec, ask yourself why the AI added it —
sometimes it's a good addition, sometimes
it's unnecessary complexityHere’s a real example of the difference in output quality:
// Vague prompt: "build me a user registration endpoint"
// Produces generic, assumption-heavy code// Specification-first prompt:
// "Build a user registration endpoint. Requirements:
// - Email must be validated and unique
// - Password minimum 8 characters, must be hashed with bcrypt
// - Rate limit to 5 attempts per 15 minutes per IP
// - Return only { id, email } in response, never the password hash"app.post('/register', registrationLimiter, async (req, res) => {
const { email, password } = req.body; if (!isValidEmail(email)) {
return res.status(400).json({ message: 'Invalid email format' });
} const existing = await User.findOne({ email });
if (existing) {
return res.status(409).json({ message: 'Email already registered' });
} const hashedPassword = await bcrypt.hash(password, 12);
const user = await User.create({ email, password: hashedPassword }); res.status(201).json({ id: user._id, email: user.email });
});The specification-first version produces code that actually matches what you need, rather than a generic guess.

3. I Stopped Trusting My First Read-Through — And Started Running a Second Pass Specifically for Assumptions
What I used to do: Read through my own hand-written code once, check that logic looked right, and move on. Since I wrote every line myself, I already understood the intent behind each decision.
Why AI broke this: When AI generates code, you didn’t make those micro-decisions yourself. A single read-through catches obvious bugs but misses baked-in assumptions — like an AI assuming an array will never be empty, or assuming a date field will always be in a specific format.
What replaced it — step by step:
Step 1: Read through the generated code once for
general logic and flow (same as before)
Step 2: Do a dedicated SECOND pass asking only:
"what is this code assuming about its input
that might not always be true?"
Step 3: For every assumption found, write a one-line
comment noting it
Step 4: Decide for each one: handle it explicitly,
or accept the risk consciously
Step 5: Never skip straight from generation to
deployment without this second passThis two-pass habit catches an entirely different category of issues than a single read-through ever did — the kind that don’t look like bugs until a specific edge case hits production.

Press enter or click to view image in full size

4. I Stopped Memorizing Syntax — And Started Memorizing Patterns Instead
What I used to do: Actively work to memorize exact syntax for common operations — array methods, regex patterns, specific library function signatures — because looking things up constantly felt inefficient.
Why AI broke this: Exact syntax is now something AI recalls instantly and correctly, every time. Continuing to memorize syntax became genuinely wasted effort. But I needed something to replace that mental energy with, or I’d end up not really learning anything at all.
What replaced it — step by step:
Step 1: When AI generates a solution, don't focus on
memorizing the exact syntax used
Step 2: Instead, identify the underlying PATTERN —
is this a debounce pattern? A memoization
pattern? A retry-with-backoff pattern?
Step 3: Name the pattern explicitly to yourself,
even if just mentally
Step 4: The next time a similar problem appears,
you'll recognize the pattern immediately,
even if you need AI to help write the
exact syntax again
Step 5: Build a personal shorthand list of patterns
you now recognize on sightThe shift is from “memorize the exact code” to “memorize the shape of the problem and its common solution family.” This turns out to be far more durable knowledge than syntax memorization ever was, and it transfers across languages and frameworks in a way that memorized syntax never could.

Press enter or click to view image in full size

5. I Stopped Committing Code the Moment It Worked — And Started Adding a Deliberate Pause Before Every Commit
What I used to do: The moment code ran successfully and passed my manual test, I’d commit it. Working code felt like a natural stopping point.
Why AI broke this: AI-generated code that “works” on the first try creates a false sense of completeness. It passed the happy path test, so it felt done — but “working” and “production-ready” are not the same thing, and this gap is wider with AI-generated code than with code you wrote and reasoned through yourself.
What replaced it — step by step:
Step 1: Code works, test passes — same as before,
but do NOT commit yet
Step 2: Add a deliberate 5-minute pause before
committing anything AI-generated
Step 3: During that pause, ask three questions:
- What happens with empty/null input?
- What happens if this runs twice simultaneously?
- What does this expose if it fails?
Step 4: Fix anything the three questions surface
Step 5: Only then commitFive minutes, three questions, every single time. This single habit change has caught more real issues in my AI-assisted code than any other single practice on this list.

Final Thoughts
None of these five changes happened because I read a productivity article and decided to adopt new habits. Each one happened because an old habit stopped making sense and I had to consciously build something to fill the gap it left behind.
That’s the part of AI-assisted coding that doesn’t get talked about enough. The tools don’t just change what you type — they change what skills you actually need to practice. Syntax memorization stopped mattering. Pattern recognition started mattering more. Reading documentation front-to-back stopped being efficient. Reading it backwards, triggered by what the AI already generated, became the better habit.
If you’ve been using AI coding tools for a while and something about your workflow feels slightly off — not broken, just off — it’s worth asking which old habit you’re still running on autopilot, and whether it actually still makes sense in the way you work now.
Five habits changed for me. There are probably more I haven’t noticed yet. That’s worth sitting with.

I’m Raj Namdev — Full Stack Developer with 5+ years of experience. I write about AI tools, the habits worth keeping, and the ones worth replacing. Follow for one new perspective every week.
